title: Focus on rebellion: Peasant revolts
description: Read passages and answer questions about peasant revolts.
topic_path: European Middle Ages: feudalism and serfdom

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

> "In 1381, English peasants had their turn in a rebellion that spread through much of England and then to London, where King Richard II tricked and murdered its leaders. The other rebels were quickly dispersed and hunted down. Similar popular uprisings occurred - and were suppressed - in the Holy Roman Empire, Hungary, Norway, Finland, Sweden, Iberia and the Netherlands. Only one major revolt was successful. (...) 
Although most of these revolts were  quickly put down, their message was not, it seems, entirely lost on the nobility. The English rebels had explicitly sought to end serfdom, and they had also questioned the social inequalities that were at the heart of aristocratic privilege. (...) These revolts, in other words, put the landed elite on notice, providing them with a good incentive to develop less exploitative methods of manorial management." 

From Judith M. Bennett and C. Warren Hollister, _Medieval Europe: A Short History_ (New York: McGraw Hill, 2006), 331-332.

**Why did the Peasants’ Revolt fail, according to the authors?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Rebellions tend to form around common causes.

choices.content: It was contained to only a small region of England
King Richard II defeated the rebel army in battle
The rebel leaders were killed
The rebels had no particular goals

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

> "In 1381, English peasants had their turn in a rebellion that spread through much of England and then to London, where King Richard II tricked and murdered its leaders. The other rebels were quickly dispersed and hunted down. Similar popular uprisings occurred - and were suppressed - in the Holy Roman Empire, Hungary, Norway, Finland, Sweden, Iberia and the Netherlands. Only one major revolt was successful. (...) 
Although most of these revolts were  quickly put down, their message was not, it seems, entirely lost on the nobility. The English rebels had explicitly sought to end serfdom, and they had also questioned the social inequalities that were at the heart of aristocratic privilege. (...) These revolts, in other words, put the landed elite on notice, providing them with a good incentive to develop less exploitative methods of manorial management." 

From Judith M. Bennett and C. Warren Hollister, _Medieval Europe: A Short History_ (New York: McGraw Hill, 2006), 331-332.

**What is the broader claim Bennett and Hollister make about popular uprisings?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Even if they would likely win, rulers preferred to avoid conflict against their subjects if possible.

choices.content: Popular uprisings were rarely successful militarily, and had no influence on the thinking of the elite
Popular uprisings were rarely successful militarily, but they did influence the thinking of the elite
Popular uprisings were typically successful militarily, but had no influence on the thinking of the elite
Popular uprisings were typically successful militarily, which is why they influenced the thinking of the elite

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

> "In 1381, English peasants had their turn in a rebellion that spread through much of England and then to London, where King Richard II tricked and murdered its leaders. The other rebels were quickly dispersed and hunted down. Similar popular uprisings occurred - and were suppressed - in the Holy Roman Empire, Hungary, Norway, Finland, Sweden, Iberia and the Netherlands. Only one major revolt was successful. (...) 
Although most of these revolts were  quickly put down, their message was not, it seems, entirely lost on the nobility. The English rebels had explicitly sought to end serfdom, and they had also questioned the social inequalities that were at the heart of aristocratic privilege. (...) These revolts, in other words, put the landed elite on notice, providing them with a good incentive to develop less exploitative methods of manorial management." 

From Judith M. Bennett and C. Warren Hollister, _Medieval Europe: A Short History_ (New York: McGraw Hill, 2006), 331-332.


**What type of evidence would support this claim?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A revolt could achieve its goals without winning a military victory.

choices.content: Changes in laws regarding the rights of peasants after revolts were suppressed
Laws remaining the same after revolts were suppressed
Records showing that more popular uprisings won military victories
Peasants gaining political offices

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

_Note: Jean Froissart, the author of this excerpt, lived from c. 1337-1405, so would have known of the Peasants’ Revolt from firsthand accounts. He worked for various nobles and royals._

> "‘Let us go to the king, he is young, and shew [show] him what servage we be in, and shew him how we will have it otherwise, or else we will provide us of some remedy [solution]; and if we go together, all manner of people that be now in any bondage will follow us to the intent to be made free; and when the king seeth us, we shall have some remedy, either by fairness or otherwise.' Thus **John Ball** said on Sundays, when the people issued out of the churches in the villages; wherefore many of the mean [common] people loved him, and such as intended to no goodness said how he said truth; and so they would murmur one with another in the fields and in the ways as they went together, affirming how John Ball said truth."

From Jean Froissart, trans. Jean Bourchier, Lord Berners, _The Chronicles of Froissart_ (New York: P.F. Collier and Son, 1910), 62-63. 


**What was John Ball’s message, according to Froissart?** 


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: John Ball was arrested multiple times for spreading ideas that challenged the way common people were treated by government officials.

choices.content: If enough common people join together, the king will have to agree to their demands
Violence was the only way to change the laws
Because the king was young, they should try to influence other royal officials instead
Common people should be more accepting of their station in life and not bother the king

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

_Note: Jean Froissart, the author of this excerpt, lived from c. 1337-1405, so would have known of the Peasants’ Revolt from firsthand accounts. He worked for various nobles and royals._

> "‘Let us go to the king, he is young, and shew [show] him what servage we be in, and shew him how we will have it otherwise, or else we will provide us of some remedy [solution]; and if we go together, all manner of people that be now in any bondage will follow us to the intent to be made free; and when the king seeth us, we shall have some remedy, either by fairness or otherwise.' Thus **John Ball** said on Sundays, when the people issued out of the churches in the villages; wherefore many of the mean [common] people loved him, and such as intended to no goodness said how he said truth; and so they would murmur one with another in the fields and in the ways as they went together, affirming how John Ball said truth."

From Jean Froissart, trans. Jean Bourchier, Lord Berners, _The Chronicles of Froissart_ (New York: P.F. Collier and Son, 1910), 62-63. 

**How did Ball’s message spread?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Many people at this time were unable to read and write.

choices.content: Through written pamphlets
Through secret meetings
Through Ball’s preaching and conversations between common people
Through a public speaking tour

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

_Note: Jean Froissart, the author of this excerpt, lived from c. 1337-1405, so would have known of the Peasants’ Revolt from firsthand accounts. He worked for various nobles and royals._

> "‘Let us go to the king, he is young, and shew [show] him what servage we be in, and shew him how we will have it otherwise, or else we will provide us of some remedy [solution]; and if we go together, all manner of people that be now in any bondage will follow us to the intent to be made free; and when the king seeth us, we shall have some remedy, either by fairness or otherwise.' Thus **John Ball** said on Sundays, when the people issued out of the churches in the villages; wherefore many of the mean [common] people loved him, and such as intended to no goodness said how he said truth; and so they would murmur one with another in the fields and in the ways as they went together, affirming how John Ball said truth."

From Jean Froissart, trans. Jean Bourchier, Lord Berners, _The Chronicles of Froissart_ (New York: P.F. Collier and Son, 1910), 62-63. 

**Given that Froissart wrote for an upper-class audience, which of the following perspectives is most likely to be present in his work?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Elites were not any more or less religious than common people.

choices.content: An excessively negative representation of common people and their motives
An overly positive representation of common people and their motives
A heavy focus on justifying the legal rights of peasants
A highly religious interpretation of the events of the revolt
